headline: Kelsey Pflendler Rows Across Pacific Solo in Record Time
meta_description: American rower Kelsey Pflendler became the fastest solo rower ever, completing the 2,400-mile California-to-Hawaii journey in 43 days.
summary_short: At 43 days, Kelsey Pflendler shattered records for the fastest solo Pacific crossing, becoming the first American woman to row alone from California to Hawaii.
body:
When Kelsey Pflendler pulled into Ala Wai Boat Harbor in Honolulu after 43 days alone at sea, she didn't just achieve a personal dream—she rewrote the record books. The American rower became the first woman of her nationality to solo row from California to Hawaii, and in doing so, set records for both the fastest solo crossing regardless of gender and the youngest woman to complete the feat.

Her 2,400-mile journey across the eastern Pacific shattered the previous women's record of 86 days, 10 hours, and 5 minutes. Remarkably, she even surpassed the men's record. The achievement gains added weight considering that just a year earlier, Pflendler had led a four-member female rowing team on the same Monterey-to-Hawaii route.

In candid vlog entries throughout the marathon row, Pflendler documented both the harrowing—rough seas and stubborn currents—and the everyday: managing sunburn and preparing meals in her 21-foot rowboat, Lily. By day 37, she found herself "grieving for the loss of this row" as the finish line approached.

Beyond the personal milestone, the expedition carried purpose. Pflendler raised $30,000 for a charity supporting the physical and mental wellbeing of Colorado River rafting guides—work she herself has done for eight years. When she arrived on her birthday, over 100 supporters gathered to celebrate.

"If any part of this made at least one person feel a little bit more powerful in their own skin," she reflected, "I'm happy."
